_note: Only documented contraindications from official labeling. Source cited per record.

contraindications.reason: Metformin is renally cleared; severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) markedly increases risk of lactic acidosis due to drug accumulation.
Hepatic impairment reduces lactate clearance, increasing the risk of lactic acidosis in patients taking metformin.
Aspirin inhibits COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis and gastric mucosal protection, exacerbating active peptic ulcers.
Inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis in 3rd trimester can cause premature closure of ductus arteriosus and delayed labour.
Atorvastatin is extensively metabolized in the liver; active liver disease or unexplained persistent elevated transaminases contraindicate use.
Statins may cause fetal harm; cholesterol is essential for fetal development. Category X in pregnancy.
Drugs acting on the RAS during 2nd and 3rd trimester can cause fetal renal dysfunction, oligohydramnios, skull hypoplasia, and neonatal death.
Telmisartan is eliminated primarily via biliary/fecal route; severe hepatic impairment dramatically increases plasma levels.
Fluoroquinolones can lower seizure threshold by antagonizing GABA-A receptors. Use with extreme caution in epileptic patients.
Not for acute bronchospasm; should not be used as monotherapy for exercise-induced bronchoconstriction if concurrent persistent asthma present.
Risk of hepatotoxicity is increased in patients with hepatic impairment; maximum dose 2g/day in severe liver disease.
Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring hepatic CYP2C19 activation; severe hepatic impairment increases bleeding risk.
Sitagliptin is renally excreted; dose must be reduced in patients with moderate-to-severe renal impairment to avoid toxicity.
Levetiracetam and its metabolite are renally eliminated; dose adjustment required for CrCl <80 mL/min.
Azithromycin can prolong QT interval; avoid in patients with known QT prolongation, bradycardia, hypokalemia, or concomitant QT-prolonging drugs.
Amiodarone contains 37% iodine by weight and can cause both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism. Contraindicated in thyroid dysfunction.

Highest teratogenicity risk among antiepileptics. Causes neural tube defects (1-2%), facial clefts, cardiac malformations, and neurodevelopmental disorders. EU Pregnancy Prevention Programme mandates specialist review.
contraindications.source_name: FDA Black Box Warning / EMA SmPC

contraindications.reason: Methotrexate is a known teratogen and abortifacient. It inhibits folic acid-dependent DNA synthesis during organogenesis causing severe birth defects.
Methotrexate is renally eliminated; impaired renal clearance causes drug accumulation and severe bone marrow toxicity.
Carbamazepine can worsen certain seizure types (absence, myoclonic). Always confirm seizure type before prescribing.

contraindications.severity: CAUTION — ABSOLUTE in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease
contraindications.reason: NSAIDs inhibit COX-1, redirecting arachidonic acid to leukotriene synthesis, triggering bronchospasm in ~10-20% of asthmatics (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease).
Beta-blockers block beta-2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, causing bronchoconstriction. Non-selective beta-blockers are contraindicated; cardioselective ones may be used cautiously.

contraindications.severity: DOSE REDUCTION REQUIRED — toxic range is narrow
contraindications.reason: Digoxin has narrow therapeutic window and is eliminated renally. Even mild renal impairment can cause digoxin toxicity. Plasma level monitoring is mandatory.
Tramadol lowers seizure threshold via opioid-independent mechanism. Contraindicated in patients with epilepsy.
Both drugs are renally excreted unchanged. All degrees of renal impairment require dose reduction based on CrCl to avoid accumulation and toxicity.
Corticosteroids cause dose-dependent hyperglycaemia. Can unmask latent diabetes. Existing diabetics require enhanced glucose monitoring and dose adjustments.
Corticosteroids impair gastric mucosal defence. Co-administration with NSAIDs synergistically increases GI bleeding risk.
In severe renal impairment, response to furosemide is blunted; higher doses may be needed. Monitor electrolytes (hyponatremia, hypokalemia risk).
Tetracyclines deposit in developing teeth and bone, causing permanent yellow-brown discolouration of teeth and inhibiting bone growth in the fetus.
SGLT-2 inhibitors require functional renal SGLT-2 transporters for glycaemic efficacy. Severely reduced in advanced CKD. Also risk of euglycaemic DKA.
